    ← Vibes

    See pricing

    Same components, three personalities -- switch modes up top ↑

    ❯ Mesh gradientVanity

    That soft, drifting glow is basically the 2020s SaaS default sky. It's pure mood -- looks current today, and it'll probably read as dated in a couple of years. Fashion, not function.

    How it's done

    Three blurred, colored blobs drift on CSS keyframes; their colors are swapped per mode via custom properties.

    Trusted by teams at

    • NORTHWIND
    • ACME
    • GLOBEX
    • INITECH
    • UMBRA
    • VANDELAY
    ❯ Logo cloudSituational

    'Trusted by' walls borrow credibility by association -- but only if the logos are real and people actually recognize them. A row of names nobody knows does nothing.

    How it's done

    A flex row of muted wordmarks. Real builds swap in licensed SVG logos.

    0Teams onboarded
    0Uptime, last 12 mo
    0Average rating
    0Requests / day
    ❯ Stat countersSituational

    Numbers ticking up read as momentum and scale -- persuasive for a B2B buyer. On a corner bakery though, they're pretty meaningless. Context is everything here.

    How it's done

    Each figure tweens from 0 to its target with GSAP when it scrolls into view, formatted with the right decimals.

    What we do

    Everything to launch and keep it running.

    Build from scratch

    Design and development from zero -- brand, front end, and the systems behind it.

    Database management

    Schema design, migrations, and ongoing care.

    Rebrand & refactor

    Bring your codebase; leave with a modern one.

    Analytics built in

    Know what works from day one.

    Secure by default

    Best-practice auth, backups, and access control.

    ❯ Bento gridSituational

    Uneven boxes of different sizes, all telling one story. Great for showing a range of features at a glance -- overuse it though, and it just becomes a cluttered quilt.

    How it's done

    CSS grid with cards spanning different column/row counts.

    ❯ Testimonial carouselSituational

    A third-party voice beats anything you could say about yourself. But auto-rotating carousels hide most of the proof -- honestly, a simple static grid of quotes often converts better anyway.

    How it's done

    Quotes cross-fade on a timer; dots let you jump. Pauses when you interact.

    How we stack up

    The chart where we win every row.

    FeatureThis productCompetitor ACompetitor B
    SSO & SAML✓✓—
    99.99% uptime SLA✓——
    24/7 human support✓✓—
    Unlimited seats✓—✓
    Transparent pricing✓——
    ❯ Comparison tableSituational

    Probably the most rigged chart in marketing -- we built it, so of course we win every row. Notice the competitor columns are vaguely named too, and conveniently missing checks. It persuades precisely because it looks objective. Fair enough when the comparison's real and the rivals are actually named; a strawman the second it isn't.

    How it's done

    A plain HTML table with our column highlighted and the feature rows chosen to flatter us. The honesty is in admitting the selection bias, not in the markup.

    Contact sales
    ❯ Pricing toggleEssential

    The monthly/annual switch is genuinely useful -- it lets buyers self-select, and nudges them toward annual with a real discount. If you're selling a subscription, you basically need this.

    How it's done

    A toggle rewrites each tier's amount and period; annual applies a 20% discount to the monthly rate.

    Questions

    Answers, folded up.

    Can I import my existing codebase?

    Yes -- bring us your current site and we'll rebrand or refactor it onto a modern stack.

    Do you manage the database too?

    We design the schema, run the migrations, and keep managing it after, if you want us to.

    How long does a build take?

    A focused marketing site is usually 2-4 weeks -- product work scales up from there.

    What happens after launch?

    We offer ongoing management, or we hand off clean, documented code -- whichever you'd rather.

    ❯ FAQ accordionEssential

    Objection-handling on demand, basically. It answers the real questions blocking a purchase without cluttering the page -- and it's genuinely accessible when it's built on native elements.

    How it's done

    Native <details>/<summary> -- keyboard-accessible and zero JavaScript.

    Offer ends in

    00:00:00
    Claim the discount
    ❯ Countdown timer⚠ Manipulative

    Real deadlines create honest urgency. But most countdowns just reset the moment they hit zero -- that's a dark pattern, and buyers increasingly see right through it. Use a true deadline, or don't use one at all.

    How it's done

    A live ticking clock. Ours honestly just counts down from three hours for the demo -- no fake reset.

    Live activity

    "Someone just bought this."

    Dana from Berlin just started a trial

    This one's caged for study. Switch to Conversion mode and a free-roaming one starts popping up in the corner of your screen -- they travel in packs with countdown timers.

    ❯ Social-proof popups⚠ Manipulative

    Those 'Ana from Austin just upgraded' toasts genuinely lift conversions -- real-time proof is persuasive. But most of them are just a plugin cycling invented names on a timer, not an actual feed of real sales. If the activity's real, it's marketing. If it's invented, it's fraud with a nice slide-in animation.

    How it's done

    A toast cycling through a hardcoded list on an interval -- which is exactly how most of these work in production. That's the point. The corner version only runs in Conversion mode.

    Someone in Austin upgraded to Growth

    Always in reach

    The bar that followed you here.

    Since the hero scrolled off screen, a slim call-to-action has been riding the bottom of your window. Scroll to the top and it goes away; scroll back down and it returns. Switch to Conversion mode and watch it get pushier -- same component, just more elbows.

    ❯ Sticky CTA barSituational

    A standing offer that shadows you down the page. Genuinely helpful when the CTA is the whole point -- saves people a scroll back up to act. It tips into nagging once it's oversized, undismissable, or covering content. Ours gets pushier in Conversion mode -- that's basically the dial between 'helpful' and 'pester.'

    How it's done

    An IntersectionObserver watches the hero; once it leaves the viewport the bar slides up. Its copy is rewritten per mode, and it remembers a dismissal for the session.

    Ready when you are.See pricing

    On the way out

    The popup that begs you to stay.

    Slide your cursor up past the top of the window, like you're reaching for the tab bar to leave. Something's going to lunge to stop you. On a phone, since that gesture doesn't exist, just tap below instead.

    ❯ Exit-intent popup⚠ Manipulative

    The modal that ambushes you right as you reach to close the tab. It rescues a few percent of leaving visitors and irritates the rest. Only honest when what it's offering is real -- an actual discount, a saved cart. A fake 'wait!' with nothing behind it just teaches people to distrust the next one.

    How it's done

    A mouseout listener watches the cursor cross the top edge of the viewport (clientY ≤ 0, no related target) and fires once per visit. It can't work on touch, so this demo adds a manual trigger.

    ⚠ you triggered an exit-intent popup

    Wait -- before you go.

    Did that stop you, even for half a second? That hesitation is basically the whole mechanism. We caught your cursor heading for the exit and threw a wall in front of it. It works -- which is exactly why it spends trust you don't get back. Use it once, for something that actually matters, or not at all.

    That's the Product Site.

    Corporate, SaaS, or conversion -- same components, three personalities.
